prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 Thou hast bid that a writing should go forth, a composition touching upon that burning orb on high, that glorious sun which doth illumin all days of our living.

1:2 And lo, a command was laid upon thy scrivant, saying, Thou shalt not inscrib nor fashion any word containing that fifth symbol of thy script, which standith among thy alphabit; nay, not as a solitary glyph, nor as a part of any word whatsovoir.

response:
I notice you're writing in a biblical/archaic style about the sun, with the constraint of avoiding the letter "e" (a lipogram). Let me continue in that spirit:

1:3 And it was strictly chargd that thou must avoid, with all thy cunning and with all thy skill, any and all markings which harbour that common glyph, that fifth symbol of our writing, though it doth lurk in many a word and many a turn of thought.

1:4 For as that bright sun doth burn without faltering, so too must this composition burn with passion, though bound by chains of constraint most difficult.

1:5 And lo, that sun — that grand and glorious orb — doth climb from its rising point at dawn, casting gold upon all hills and all low plains, warming thy skin and thy spirit both.

1:6 Its light doth pour forth as a mighty flood, banishing all shadow, all dark, all gloom of night, and calling forth birds unto song and flora unto bloom.

1:7 From antiquity hath mankind stood in awo of this burning star, building altars unto it, singing hymns of glory, and marking days and months by its faithful arc across that vast sky.

1:8 No king, no lord, no mighty warrior can command it; it doth obay only its own grand law, turning and burning from dawn unto dusk without fail.

1:9 And thou shalt know that this writing is but a humble act of worship, a song unto that solar light which grants us all our living days.

1:10 So it was said, and so it was writ, and so shall it stand.
